steps.title: Identify the angle relationships using parallel lines
steps.content:
Lines $q$ and $t$ are parallel, and two transversals $r$ and $s$ cross both parallel lines.

The angle $a = 43°$ is formed at the intersection of transversal $r$ with line $q$.

The angle $b = 122°$ is formed at the intersection of transversal $s$ with line $q$, measured as an exterior angle on that side. The interior angle at that intersection (on the side toward the triangle) is:
$$180° - b = 180° - 122° = 58°$$

steps.title: Apply the Exterior Angle Theorem to find the angle at the apex
steps.content:
Transversals $r$ and $s$ meet at a point $P$ above line $q$, forming a triangle with two vertices on line $q$.

Let the interior angles of this triangle at the two vertices on $q$ be $a = 43°$ and $58°$ (as found above). By the triangle angle-sum theorem, the apex angle at $P$ is:
$$\angle P = 180° - 43° - 58° = 79°$$

Alternatively, by the Exterior Angle Theorem, the exterior angle at the vertex where $b$ is marked equals the sum of the two non-adjacent interior angles:
$$b = 122° = a + \angle P \Rightarrow \angle P = 122° - 43° = 79°$$

steps.title: Use co-interior angles and the angle at P to find w
steps.content:
Because $q \parallel t$, the transversal $r$ creates co-interior (same-side interior) angles with the two parallel lines. The angle that $r$ makes with line $t$ on the interior side is:
$$180° - a = 180° - 43° = 137°$$

Similarly, the transversal $s$ makes a co-interior angle with line $t$ of:
$$180° - 58° = 122°$$

The angle $w$ is at the intersection inside line $t$ between the two transversals. Using the exterior angle of the triangle formed below line $t$: the exterior angle at one base equals the sum of the two remote interior angles. The two base interior angles contributed by $r$ and $s$ at line $t$ are $43°$ and $58°$ (alternate interior angles with the angles at $q$). So the angle between the transversals at line $t$, measured as an exterior angle, is:
$$a + (180° - b) = 43° + 58° = 101°$$

The angle $w$ is the angle on line $t$ between the two transversals, which is half of $101°$ because $w$ is the angle between one transversal and the bisecting direction, or equivalently, $w$ equals the mean of the two alternate interior angles:
$$w = \frac{a + (180° - b)}{2} = \frac{43° + 58°}{2} = \frac{101°}{2} = 50.5°$$
